items.q: Why did the Provisional Government fail in 1917?
items.correct: It continued the unpopular war, did not redistribute land, shared 'dual power' with the Petrograd Soviet, and was discredited by the Kornilov affair before the Bolshevik takeover.
items.misconception: The Provisional Government fell because Lenin was personally more charismatic than Kerensky.
items.random_wrong: The Provisional Government fell because the Tsar returned and reclaimed his throne.
items.why: Its failure was structural, not personal: war, land and dual power. Students reduce political collapse to charismatic leadership when material grievances drove events.

items.q: What did Lenin's April Theses demand in 1917?
items.correct: No support for the Provisional Government, all power to the Soviets, end the war, redistribute land — slogans summarised as 'Peace, Bread, Land'.
items.misconception: Lenin's April Theses called for an immediate alliance with the Provisional Government.
items.random_wrong: The April Theses created the Soviet Union.
items.why: Lenin uniquely demanded the Bolsheviks reject the Provisional Government when others wanted cooperation. Students miss how radical this position was even among Bolsheviks.

items.q: What happened in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.correct: There were two revolutions: February overthrew the Tsar and installed the Provisional Government; October saw the Bolsheviks seize power from that government.
items.misconception: The Bolsheviks overthrew the Tsar in 1917.
items.random_wrong: Lenin personally led the storming of the Winter Palace.
items.why: Students collapse 1917 into one event. The Tsar abdicated in February under pressure from liberals and soldiers — the Bolsheviks took power eight months later from a republican government.
It's Lenin. “Trotsky” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What discredited the Provisional Government in June 1917?
items.why: It's The Kerensky Offensive. “The Kornilov Affair” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which month did the July Days occur (1917)?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
